1: Samuel takes a flask of oil (pak haššemen), pours it on Saul's head, kisses him, and says: 'Has YHWH not anointed you as ruler (nāgîd) over his heritage (naḥălāṯô)?' The anointing with oil (māšaḥ) is the act that makes Saul māšîaḥ — anointed one. The vocabulary connects to royal and priestly anointing across the Torah and is the root of the later Hebrew māšîaḥ and Greek christos. Samuel's kiss is a covenant gesture — the kiss in the ANE signals the sealing of a relationship, not merely affection. YHWH's heritage is naḥălāh — inheritance/patrimony — Israel seen as YHWH's personal possession.
2: The first confirming sign: two men at Rachel's tomb in Benjaminite territory at Zelzah will tell Saul the donkeys have been found and that his father is worried about him. Rachel's tomb (near Bethlehem or at Zelzah in Benjamin — the location is debated) is a patriarchal landmark. The sign is mundane and verifiable — Saul can check it immediately. The three signs move from the mundane (donkeys found) to the sacred (Spirit-filled prophecy), scaling up toward the transformative.
3: The second sign: three men going up to God at Bethel, carrying young goats, three loaves of bread, and a skin of wine. One will greet Saul and give him two loaves of bread, which he will accept. Bethel is a pilgrimage site (one of Israel's major sanctuaries before Jerusalem); the three men are on a cultic journey. Saul receives bread — the provision confirms his appointment. The bread gift anticipates the sacrificial meal at Gibeah, where the Spirit-event will occur.
4: The men will greet Saul and offer two loaves of bread, which he should accept from them. The dual loaves (šənê leḥem) may echo the double portion of the firstborn or the elevated portion; accepting the gift is an act of receptivity that positions Saul as one who receives provision for the task rather than providing it. The three-sign structure moves incrementally: first domestic news (donkeys), then cultic provision (bread), then prophetic transformation (Spirit).
5: The third sign: at the hill of God (giḇʿaṯ hāʾĕlōhîm), where the Philistine garrison is, a band of prophets coming down from the high place with harp, tambourine, flute, and lyre, prophesying. The location is charged: the garrison of the Philistine overlords is at the same place where YHWH's prophetic band operates. The juxtaposition — military occupation and prophetic worship in the same location — is the social landscape of early monarchy.
6: 'The Spirit of YHWH will rush upon you (ṣālaḥ), and you will prophesy with them and be turned into another man (wənehepaḵtā ləʾîš ʾaḥēr).' The verb ṣālaḥ denotes a sudden, powerful onset — the same word used when the Spirit came on Samson (Judges 14:6,19; 1 Samuel 15:14). The transformation into 'another man' is the most striking phrase: it describes a fundamental reorientation of character, not merely a temporary prophetic episode. This is the OT's language for Spirit-transformation.
7: 'When these signs come to you, do what the occasion requires, for God is with you.' The freedom granted here — 'do what the occasion requires' (ʿăśēh-ləḵā ʾăšer timmāṣēʾ yāḏeḵā, lit. 'do what your hand finds') — is a significant latitude for the newly anointed one. Samuel is not micromanaging; he is authorizing Saul to act with royal initiative once the Spirit confirms the appointment. The formula 'God is with you' is the guarantee that grounds the freedom.
8: Samuel instructs Saul to go ahead to Gilgal and wait seven days for Samuel to come, offer burnt offerings, and sacrifice peace offerings. This instruction is fateful — Saul's later failure in 1 Samuel 13:8-14 occurs precisely when he fails to wait the full seven days. The instruction is both practical (establishing Gilgal as the cultic center for military campaign) and a test: will Saul trust Samuel's timeline when Philistine pressure mounts?
9: 'As he turned his back to leave Samuel, God gave him another heart (wəhāpak-lô ʾĕlōhîm lēḇ ʾaḥēr).' The divine transformation happens immediately at Saul's departure — not at some later date. The 'other heart' corresponds to the 'other man' of v6: both describe the Spirit-effected change that accompanies anointing. All the signs came that day, confirming that Samuel's word was accurate and that divine endorsement was underway.
10: At Gibeah, the band of prophets meets Saul and the Spirit of God rushes on him (ṣālaḥ), and he prophesies among them. The third sign is fulfilled exactly as Samuel described. Saul is caught up in the prophetic band's Spirit-empowered activity. Prophecy here is not necessarily verbal proclamation; the Hebrew hitnabbēʾ can describe ecstatic behavior, song, and Spirit-inspired movement — a whole-person experience of divine presence.
11: Those who had known Saul previously see him prophesying among the prophets and say: 'What has happened to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets (hagam šāʾûl bannəḇîʾîm)?' The question expresses surprise: Saul is not from the prophetic circles, not a trained member of the prophetic band. The Spirit's work exceeds social categories — it falls on a Benjaminite farmer-warrior, not on professional prophets. The question becomes a proverb (see v12).
12: A man from that area responds: 'And who is their father?' — a rhetorical question implying the prophets have no single parentage, that the Spirit makes prophets without hereditary line. The saying 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' becomes a proverbial expression in Israel, cited again in 1 Samuel 19:24. The proverb encodes the lesson: the Spirit moves sovereignly, outside conventional categories of lineage and training.
13: After the prophetic episode, Saul comes to the high place. The transition back to ordinary movement — from ecstatic Spirit experience to walking up to the high place — marks the temporary nature of this initial Spirit-coming. Unlike the anointing's 'other heart' (v9), the prophetic rush was an episode within a larger process of confirmation, not the permanent Spirit-indwelling of 1 Samuel 16 which is then definitively withdrawn in 1 Samuel 16:14.
14: Saul's uncle asks him where he went. Saul tells him: he went to look for the donkeys and found them with Samuel. The uncle presses: 'Tell me what Samuel said to you.' The question tests whether Saul will reveal the anointing. His response is instructive.
15: 'Please tell me what Samuel said to you.' The uncle's curiosity is genuine but Saul's discretion in response (v16) is notable. The uncle-nephew relationship was significant in Benjaminite tribal structures; Saul's uncle may have been monitoring family developments.
16: Saul tells his uncle they found the donkeys — and says nothing about the kingship. 'But about the matter of the kingdom, of which Samuel had spoken, he did not tell him anything.' The restraint is theologically significant: Saul does not yet act on the anointing, does not claim the kingship, does not announce himself. This discretion contrasts with later Saul, who will act precipitously. The concealment preserves YHWH's chosen moment for the public revelation.
17: Samuel summons the people to YHWH at Mizpah. Mizpah (meaning 'watchtower') in Benjamin was a major assembly point — used for the anti-Benjamin war (Judg 20-21) and now for the formal installation of the king. Samuel convenes the national assembly as YHWH's prophet, giving the event theocratic legitimacy. The choice of Mizpah connects Saul's installation to the site of earlier national crisis.
18: Samuel's preamble rehearses YHWH's saving acts: 'I brought Israel up from Egypt and delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of all the kingdoms that oppressed you.' The covenant-recital formula before the lot-casting reminds Israel whose authority governs even the selection of a king. The king they are about to install is still YHWH's choice — the lot will confirm it — but YHWH's prior claim as deliverer must be acknowledged first.
19: 'But today you have rejected your God, who saves you from all your evils and your distresses, and you have said to him, Set a king over us.' Samuel's indictment is sharp: the act of asking for a king, even before a king has done anything wrong, is already a rejection of YHWH as king. The people are not rejecting bad leadership; they are rejecting the covenant structure in which YHWH leads through charismatic judges and prophets. Despite this, YHWH concedes the request.
20: The lot-casting by tribes: Samuel presents all the tribes and Benjamin is chosen. The Hebrew lākaḏ (taken/caught by lot) is used for the lot-selection process — the lot 'takes' the tribe, then the clan, then the household, then the man. This sequential narrowing from tribe to individual enacts YHWH's sovereignty at every level of social organization.
21: The Matrites are taken from Benjamin, and from the Matrite family Saul son of Kish is taken. But when they seek him, he is not found. Saul hides among the baggage (baḵkēlîm). The image of the king-elect hiding in the luggage is one of the most peculiar in the narrative. Commentators debate whether this is fear, false modesty, or a kind of ritual withdrawal. The hiding emphasizes that Saul is not self-promoting — he must be extracted rather than presenting himself.
22: They ask YHWH: 'Is there a man still to come here?' YHWH answers: 'He has hidden himself among the baggage.' Even Saul's hiding place is within YHWH's knowledge. The oracle is laconic — no explanation of why Saul hid — but confirms that YHWH's chosen one and the lot-selection agree. The lot is not mechanical chance; it discloses YHWH's predetermined selection.
23: They run and bring Saul out, and he stands among the people taller than all the people from his shoulders upward — the physical description of 9:2 is repeated. The narrative returns to the height marker at the moment of public presentation: this is the external quality Israel had in mind when they asked for a king 'like all the nations.' YHWH grants them a king who fits their category, knowing the deeper failure that will follow.
24: Samuel says to all the people: 'Do you see him whom YHWH has chosen? There is none like him among all the people.' And all the people shouted, 'Long live the king!' (yəḥî hammelek). The acclamation formula is the formal public installation. Samuel's rhetorical question — 'Do you see him?' — inverts the seeing-motif: Israel sees the tall external form; YHWH has seen differently. The crowd's unanimous endorsement completes the installation that YHWH's private anointing began.
25: Samuel explains to the people the rights and duties of the kingship (mišpaṭ hamməlûḵāh), writes them in a scroll, and places it before YHWH. The written record of constitutional limits on kingship is a remarkable act: the monarchy is inaugurated with a constitutional document from the start. This 'law of the king' may be related to Deuteronomy 17:14-20's royal law. Writing it 'before YHWH' (i.e., deposited at the sanctuary) gives it covenant-treaty status.
26: Saul goes home to Gibeah, and with him go the 'sons of valor' (ḥayil) whose hearts God had touched. The emergence of a voluntary honor guard before any formal military appointment is another sign of divine orchestration: the people who attach themselves to Saul are those whose hearts God has disposed toward him. The kingdom forms organically around the anointed one before any institutional structure is in place.
27: But some worthless men (bənê bəlîyyaʿal) despise Saul, saying 'How can this man save us?' and bring him no gift. Saul holds his peace. The bənê bəlîyyaʿal — sons of worthlessness/Belial — are the counter-voice in every pivotal moment of the Deuteronomistic History (cf. Judges 19:22; 1 Samuel 2:12). Their question — 'How can this man save us?' — is not merely disrespect; it questions the entire enterprise of YHWH's appointed deliverer. Saul's silence in response to mockery contrasts with his later rash reactions.
